query_text:
It will be seen that this mere painstaking burrower and grub-worm of
  a poor devil of a Sub-Sub appears to have gone through the long
  Vaticans and street-stalls of the earth, picking up whatever random
  allusions to whales he could anyways find in any book whatsoever,
  sacred or profane.
label: verbatim
notes: gutenberg:2701 — opens "It will be seen that this mere painstaking"

query_text:
I try in vain to be persuaded that the pole is the seat of
frost and desolation; it ever presents itself to my imagination as the
region of beauty and delight. There, Margaret, the sun is for ever
visible, its broad disk just skirting the horizon and diffusing a
perpetual splendour.
label: verbatim
notes: gutenberg:84 — opens "I try in vain to be persuaded"

query_text:
Two households, both alike in dignity,
In fair Verona, where we lay our scene,
From ancient grudge break to new mutiny,
Where civil blood makes civil hands unclean.
From forth the fatal loins of these two foes
A pair of star-cross’d lovers take their life;
label: verbatim
notes: gutenberg:1513 — opens "Two households, both alike in dignity"

query_text:
Walt Whitman has somewhere a fine and just distinction between “loving
by allowance” and “loving with personal love.” This distinction applies
to books as well as to men and women; and in the case of the not very
numerous authors who are the objects of the personal affection, it
brings a curious consequence with it.
label: verbatim
notes: gutenberg:1342 — opens "Walt Whitman has somewhere a fine and just"

query_text:
It will be seen that this mere painstaking burrower and earth-worm of
  a poor devil of a Sub-Sub appears to have gone through the long
  Vaticans and street stalls of the earth, picking up whatever random
  allusions to whales he could always find in any book whatsoever,
  sacred or profane.
label: light_edit
notes: gutenberg:2701 — "It will be seen that this mere painstaking"; edits: grub-worm→earth-worm, street-stalls→street stalls, anyways→always

query_text:
I try in vain to be persuaded that the pole is the seat of
frost and desolation; it ever presents itself to my imagination as the
region of beauty and delight. There, Margaret, the sun is forever
visible, its wide disk just skirting the horizon and diffusing a
constant splendour.
label: light_edit
notes: gutenberg:84 — "I try in vain to be persuaded"; edits: for ever→forever, broad disk→wide disk, perpetual→constant

query_text:
Two households, both alike in dignity,
In fair Verona, where we set our scene,
From ancient grudge break to new mutiny,
Where civil blood makes civil hands unclean.
From forth the fatal loins of these two foes
A pair of star-crossed lovers take their life;
label: light_edit
notes: gutenberg:1513 — "Two households, both alike in dignity"; edits: 'lay our scene'→'set our scene', star-cross’d→star-crossed

query_text: It will be observed that this mere industrious burrower and grub-worm of a wretched scoundrel of a Sub-Sub appears to have scoured the great libraries and bookstalls of the world, gathering whatever chance allusions to whales he could possibly locate in any text whatsoever, holy or secular. Therefore one must not, in every instance, take these disorganized whale statements, however genuine, in these extracts, for true gospel cetology.
label: paraphrase
notes: gutenberg:2701 — "It will be seen that this mere painstaking"; clumsy synonym swap: seen→observed, Vaticans/street-stalls→libraries/bookstalls, poor devil→wretched scoundrel, higgledy-piggledy→disorganized, veritable→true

query_text: My imagination refuses to picture the pole as a place of frost and desolation, however much I try to convince myself otherwise. Beauty and delight are what it conjures instead. Margaret, the sun is always on the horizon there, its broad disk never setting, filling the world with unceasing light. And—trusting the accounts of those who sailed before—snow and frost are said to vanish there; a calm sea carries you toward a land of wonders exceeding anything yet found on this inhabited earth.
label: paraphrase
notes: gutenberg:84 — "I try in vain to be persuaded that the pole"; main clause inverted (imagination as topic sentence), temporal connectives reshuffled, perpetual splendour rephrased as 'unceasing light'

query_text: The story unfolds in Verona, where two families of equal standing have long nursed a hatred that now flares again into open violence. Blood is shed by citizens who had been peaceful neighbors. From these two rival houses are born a pair of ill-fated lovers who, through their tragic deaths, bring the bitter quarrel between their parents to an end.
label: paraphrase
notes: gutenberg:1513 — "Two households, both alike in dignity"; Chorus address (2nd-person, verse) rewritten as third-person narrative prose; active voice flipped to passive in places; no archaic diction retained

query_text: Whitman once drew a distinction between affection that is granted by convention and affection that is genuine and personal. This idea applies as readily to books as to people. For those relatively few authors who inspire real personal devotion in their readers, an odd side effect follows: their admirers tend to disagree far more sharply about which book is the author's greatest achievement than is the case with writers who are merely admired out of habit.
label: paraphrase
notes: gutenberg:1342 — "Walt Whitman has somewhere a fine and just distinction"; two source sentences collapsed; Whitman's quoted terms ('loving by allowance', 'loving with personal love') replaced with prose paraphrases; Austenians omitted

query_text: When a query planner encounters a large table scan, it weighs the cost of a sequential scan against that of an index seek. For tables with low selectivity—where the predicate matches most rows—the planner often prefers the sequential scan because random I/O from repeated index lookups exceeds the cost of reading pages in order.
label: negative_offtopic
notes: Database query-planner optimization. Entirely unrelated to the literary corpus.

query_text: Sourdough fermentation depends on a stable community of wild yeasts and lactic acid bacteria living in the starter. During bulk fermentation, the dough should roughly double in volume; the timing varies with ambient temperature, typically running from four to twelve hours at room temperature. Fold the dough every thirty minutes for the first two hours to build tension.
label: negative_offtopic
notes: Sourdough bread baking. Entirely unrelated to any corpus document.

query_text: Humpback whales are among the most vocal creatures in the ocean. Males produce elaborate songs that can last for hours, gradually evolving as the season progresses until all bulls in a given ocean basin are singing the same updated version. These acoustic displays appear to function primarily in mate competition rather than in direct communication with females.
label: negative_same_topic
notes: About humpback whale communication—same subject (whales) as gutenberg:2701 (Moby-Dick). Written independently; discusses cetacean biology rather than the Sub-Sub's cataloguing work. No phrasing derived from corpus.

query_text: Jane Austen's six completed novels are remarkable for the precision of their social observation and the sharpness of their irony. Writing during the Regency period, she mapped the anxieties of the English gentry with a comic touch that made her work feel both timely and enduring. Among her novels, Pride and Prejudice has consistently drawn the widest readership.
label: negative_same_topic
notes: About Jane Austen's novels—same subject as gutenberg:1342. Written independently; focuses on Austen's style and readership history, not the Whitman distinction about personal vs. conventional love. No 5-gram overlap expected.

query_text: Romeo and Juliet has been staged continuously since the late sixteenth century, making it one of the most performed plays in the English language. Directors have set it in periods ranging from Renaissance Italy to contemporary urban settings, finding that the conflict between family loyalty and individual desire translates easily across eras.
label: negative_same_topic
notes: About the performance history of Romeo and Juliet—same subject as gutenberg:1513. Focuses on staging history rather than plot to avoid echoing the prologue. FLAG: 'conflict between family loyalty and individual desire' is thematically parallel to the prologue but no 5-gram overlap; verify.
